title: How trauma changes the way the brain processes time
author: Kalesh
excerpt: You know how people say time heals all wounds? I used to believe that. I wanted to believe it. But here's the thing: trauma doesn't just hurt you in the moment...
metaDescription: You know how people say time heals all wounds? I used to believe that. I wanted to believe it. But here's the thing: trauma doesn't just hurt you in the m...
htmlContent:
You know how people say time heals all wounds? I used to believe that. I wanted to believe it. But here's the thing: trauma doesn't just hurt you in the moment. It rewires the entire way your brain experiences time itself. Your past doesn't stay in the past. Your future feels impossible to reach. And the present? It might not even feel real.

I have sat with this reality for years now. Both in my own body and in conversations with countless others. And what I have found is that trauma doesn't just distort memory. It changes your relationship with time. It bends it. It breaks it. It makes it something you can no longer trust.

Let's talk about what actually happens in the brain when trauma messes with your sense of time. Because understanding this can be the first step toward getting your life back.

The collapsing of past and present

Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote the book on trauma and the body, has spent decades studying this. He found that trauma disrupts the brain's ability to distinguish between past and present. Your hippocampus, the part of your brain that helps you locate events in time, literally shrinks under chronic stress. Think about that. Your brain's timekeeper gets damaged.

So when you have a flashback, you are not just remembering something bad. Your brain is reliving it. Right now. In this moment. The same neural pathways fire. The same stress hormones flood your system. Your body believes the threat is still happening, even if the actual event happened years ago.

I remember sitting in my therapist's office describing a moment from my childhood. I was telling the story calmly. Then suddenly I wasn't calm anymore. My heart was racing. My hands were shaking. I was right back there, age seven, terrified. My therapist gently said, "Notice what just happened in your body. You left this room."

She was right. I had traveled through time without moving an inch. That is what trauma does. It collapses the boundary between then and now. Your past becomes your present. And your present becomes a haunted house you can never fully leave.

This is why standard advice like "just let it go" or "move on" feels so insulting to trauma survivors. It's not that we don't want to move on. It's that our brains literally cannot tell the difference between then and now. The past is alive inside us. It breathes. It grabs us by the throat when we least expect it.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link). Van der Kolk's work helped pave the way for this kind of understanding, and that book builds on it in a way that feels human and accessible.

Why the future feels impossible

Here is another strange thing trauma does to time. It makes the future feel like a lie. You cannot plan ahead. You cannot imagine yourself next week, next month, next year. The future feels like a blank wall or a black hole. It does not exist.

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed polyvagal theory, would explain this through the lens of your nervous system. When you are stuck in a survival state, your brain shuts down future-oriented thinking. Why would your brain waste energy on planning a vacation or a career when it believes you might die in the next five minutes? Survival mode is not creative. It is not hopeful. It is a tight, narrow tunnel with only one exit: immediate safety.

I have talked to people who say they cannot even make small plans. A coffee date next week feels impossible. Buying concert tickets three months from now feels absurd. They have learned, through trauma, that the future is unreliable. That promises get broken. That safety can disappear in an instant. So their brain refuses to engage with a future it cannot trust.

This is not laziness. This is not a character flaw. This is your nervous system doing exactly what it was designed to do: protect you from disappointment and danger. But the cost is enormous. Without a sense of future, you lose motivation. You lose hope. You lose the ability to build a life that feels like it is going somewhere.

If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in It Didn't Start with You (paid link). It explores how trauma can echo through generations, shaping your relationship with time and possibility before you even had a choice in the matter.

The frozen present and the feeling of timelessness

Then there is the other side of this coin. Some trauma survivors get stuck in a different kind of time distortion. The present becomes an endless, frozen moment. Nothing changes. Nothing moves. You are just existing, day after day, in a gray, timeless haze.

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, has written extensively about this. He calls it the freeze response. When your nervous system decides that fighting or fleeing is impossible, it shuts down. Your body goes limp. Your mind goes numb. Time stops because movement stops. And without movement, there is no progression. No before. No after. Just a stuck, suspended now.

I have been there. I know what it feels like to wake up and realize three months have passed and you cannot remember a single day. Everything blends together. The hours are long but the weeks are short. You are living in a strange paradox where time both drags and disappears.

This is not mindfulness. This is not being present. This is being trapped in a present that has no texture, no color, no future, and no past that feels safe to touch. It is a kind of living death that the wellness industry does not talk about enough. They tell you to be present without understanding that for some people, the present is the most dangerous place to be.

Sit with that. If you have ever felt this way, you are not broken. You are not failing at meditation. Your nervous system is protecting you from a reality that feels too overwhelming to fully inhabit.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link). It offers concrete tools for gently coaxing yourself out of that frozen, timeless state without forcing or pushing.

Healing your relationship with time

So what do you do when trauma has broken your sense of time? How do you begin to repair something that feels so fundamental?

First, you stop trying to force it. You cannot bully your nervous system into feeling safe with time. You cannot tell your brain "just be normal about the future" and expect it to cooperate. Healing time perception requires a slow, bottom-up approach. You have to work with the body, not against it.

One thing that helps is grounding in the present moment through sensation, not thought. Instead of trying to convince yourself that you are safe, you let your body feel safety through small, repeatable actions. Touching something soft. Feeling your feet on the floor. Noticing the temperature of the air. These tiny sensory anchors help your brain slowly rebuild a sense of now that is not threatening.

Another thing is building small, predictable routines. Your brain needs evidence that the future is reliable. It needs proof. So you start with something tiny. A cup of tea at the same time each morning. A five minute walk after lunch. A bedtime ritual that never changes. These small patterns tell your nervous system: the future can be counted on. The future will show up.

It takes time. It takes patience. And it takes accepting that your relationship with time may never look like someone else's. You might always have a different sense of past, present, and future. That is okay. That is not failure. That is adaptation.

For those looking for gentle nervous system support, something worth trying is NOW L-Theanine 200mg (paid link). It can take the edge off the hypervigilance that keeps your brain stuck in survival time.

Healing your relationship with time is not about forgetting the past. It is about giving the past its proper place. Not as something that happens to you every day. But as something that happened. Something that shaped you. Something that does not have to own your present or steal your future.

You deserve to experience time as something that moves. Something that changes. Something that carries you forward, not just holds you down. And that is possible. Even when it does not feel like it right now.

I am still learning this myself. Some days I feel time flowing. Other days I am back in that frozen, timeless space. But the good days are getting more frequent. The future is starting to feel real again. And that is enough for now.
